    {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_33_the-hidden-weapon-against-the-youtube-algorithm",
      "title": "How Archimedes Almost Broke Math with Circles",
      "framework": "How (Authority Figure) Almost Broke (Entity) with (Unexpected Entity)",
      "hook_score": 192929,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "Negativity – There’s drama here with “Almost <em>Broke</em> Math”. Curiosity – This title is counterintuitive — it’s not possible to break math, right? And almost breaking something unbreakable (math) with something so simple (circles) has a bit of contrast. Authority – Dropping Archimedes’ name gives credibility to this title. Btw, I asked <a href=\"https://twitter.com/BenSyversen\">​Ben​</a>, the creator of this video, about how he came up with the title and here’s what he said: “An earlier draft was “How the Area of a Circle Almost Broke Math.” After showing a draft of the thumbnail to a friend, I realized that the video needed to be not just about circles but also about Archimedes, so I changed the title and built out the script accordingly. It was my first time really trying to do title and thumbnail first, and in this case, getting clarity on the title/thumbnail combo really gave me a lot of clarity on the direction that the video needed to go. I didn’t realize that would happen, but it’s sold me on the idea of starting with the title and thumbnail!” <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Tell the story of how somebody almost broke something unbreakable. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong>",
      "why_it_flopped": "",
      "view_count": "",
      "created_at": "2026-03-16 21:40:33"
    },

      "newsletter": "newsletter_15_huge-lies-youtube-gurus-are-telling-you",
      "title": "Someone Called the GAME WARDEN Because I Was Catching Giant Fish!",
      "framework": "Someone Called the (Authorities) Because I Was (Awesome Activity)!",
      "hook_score": 57894,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "Authority – Calling the game warden increases the magnitude of what’s going on here. Negativity – This title promises a lot of drama, which people love. Desire – This creator’s audience would love to catch such big fish that somebody calls the game warden on them. Curiosity – “Giant Fish” and the thumbnail only showing a splash and an arrow build curiosity because they make you wonder what type of fish was caught. <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Tell your audience about a time when you did something so awesome somebody called the authorities. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong>",
      "why_it_flopped": "",
      "view_count": "",
      "created_at": "2026-03-17 12:33:58"
    },

      "title": "Luxury Fashion Is For Broke People",

      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "Curiosity – This title builds curiosity two ways: Negativity – There’s a bit of drama and controversy here by saying luxury fashion is for broke people. <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Say that something on one end of the spectrum is actually for people on the other end of the spectrum. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong>",

    {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_167_i-created-an-anonymous-youtube-channel-to-prove-its-not-luck",
      "title": "Struggles of African Immigrant Working in Canada",
      "framework": "Struggles of (Specific Situation)",
      "hook_score": 39833,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "This video was a massive breakout from a new channel and I’m not *exactly* sure why it did so well, but I have a few theories: Negativity – People love negativity and drama. Talking about struggles and how a country isn’t worth it definitely fits that. Confirmation – A lot of people in the comments are essentially saying “Yes, I know! This has been my experience too.” When you confirm what lots of people are thinking, but may not be openly talked about, they’ll want to watch and comment. Specific Audience – When you go too specific on YouTube it’s easy to have too small of a potential audience. But people living in Canada, Africans, and immigrants seem to rally around this video saying this is what they’re feeling. If you (and the YouTube algorithm) can find a specific audience that has strong feelings about your content, you can win big. <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Talk about the struggles of a specific group of people. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong> P.S. Big thanks to this creator, Iram, who reads Creator Hooks and sent this to me!",
      "why_it_flopped": "",
      "view_count": "",
      "created_at": "2026-03-17 12:34:19"
    },

      "newsletter": "newsletter_45_i-tried-30-youtube-strategies-these-5-actually-worked",

List – Lists make videos feel more tangible and add some curiosity. <strong>How you can use this hook:</strong> Address a big paint point for your audience, then tell them about the common mistakes to avoid. <strong>Examples of this hook in action:</strong>",

    {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_22_what-i-learned-posting-100-videos-in-100-days",
      "title": "What I learned approaching 1000 women in 1000 days",
      "framework": "What I learned (Activity for a large number of times) in (Time Frame)",
      "hook_score": 17919,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "Desire – This creator’s audience wants to learn more about talking to women. Credibility – This isn’t just him preaching about how to talk to girls, he’s sharing lessons he learned by actually getting out there and talking to girls. Time Frame – The time frame of 1000 days makes this video feel more tangible, real, and interesting. <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Do something a lot of times in a specific time frame to build credibility and share what you learned. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong> Alrighty, this week we’re trying something new! Instead of the normal Flop of the Week, this week we have the Click Trigger of the Week. Click triggers are, well, triggers that make people click. You can see different click triggers in the “Why this works” sections below each video in this newsletter. This week’s Click Trigger of the Week is Credibility. When you show credibility in your title you give people a reason to trust your content. You can this by doing an experiment, mentioning what you’ve accomplished, or interviewing people who are experienced in your video’s topic. Anybody can make a video about a topic the audience is interested in, but you’re way more likely to win the click if you show why you’re worth listening to. And this will become even more important as AI becomes more prevalent and anybody can use it to throw together a script and make a video. The video just above about talking to 1000 girls in 1000 days is an example of this, but here are a few others: <a href=\"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RpsOW3NjXvQ\"></a>NYC Cab Drivers eat in NYC every day, so they have credibility when it comes to knowing the local food spots. <a href=\"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TAkGO2oa0Qs\"></a>This guy could have made a video about why being a successful musician isn’t about luck, but instead, he <em>showed</em> you that it’s not about luck by doing an experiment. <a href=\"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9X8M7ENDlJ8\"></a>She could have just said, “Celebrity House Tours Are Full of Lies,” but instead she gave us a reason to trust her by telling us she watched 151 tours. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.     {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_164_the-one-choice-that-will-determine-your-youtube-channels-success",
      "title": "Getting ADDICTED to STUDYING is Easy, Actually",
      "framework": "Getting ADDICTED to (Beneficial, but hard activity) is Easy, Actually",
      "hook_score": 17518,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "Desire – This creator’s audience wants to study more. The confident tone of this title makes you feel like this video can help you. Curiosity – Many people struggle with studying, so saying it’s “Easy, Actually,” is counterintuitive. Controversy – Saying getting “ADDICTED” to studying is more bold, polarizing, and controversial than just, “studying more,” so it grabs more attention. Contrast – The contrast between “ADDICTED” (negative connotation) and “STUDYING” (positive connotation) makes this title more interesting. <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Tell your audience that getting addicted to something beneficial, but is sometimes hard to do, is easy, actually. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong>",
      "why_it_flopped": "",

      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "Curiosity – This builds curiosity in two ways: Fear – Nobody wants to lose out on thousands of dollars from a hidden rule. <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Tell your audience about a hidden rule with a big cost that most miss. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong>",

    {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_35_5-youtube-title-formulas-you-need-to-know",
      "title": "42 STITCHES You Need To Know And Wardrobe ORGANIZATION Ideas",
      "framework": "42 (Specific moves) You Need To Know And (Goal) Ideas",
      "hook_score": 13571,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "List – Lists make videos feel more tangible (you know exactly what the video will be about), and they add some curiosity. Desire – This creator’s audience is into stitches and wardrobe organization. Specific – This is about specific stitches, which are very tangible and feel like they’re likely to be helpful. This not the case for abstract things like tips, which may or may not be helpful. Note: I’m typically not a fan of having 2 subjects in a title. I wonder if this video would have done just as well (or even better) if the second part, “And Wardrobe ORGANIZATION Ideas,” was deleted. <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Tell your audience about specific, tangible things they need to know. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong> Here’s a simple test: comparing “$1.5 Million” vs. “$1,500,000”. My hypothesis was that all the zeroes would grab people’s attention and really show how big a number $1,500,000 is. I’m not saying it always works, but it did work here. It also simplified the title to only focus on the coolest part of the video and not ask a potentially distracting question. (This is the reasoning behind my extra note in the breakdown of the title above.) A question for you this week as you’re writing your titles: How can you show (not just tell) how interesting your video is? If you want to see 100+ other test results like this, you can <a href=\"https://go.creatorhooks.com/103-youtube-title-a-b-tests\">​​​​​​get the 103 YouTube AB tests ebook here​​​​​​</a>.       "why_it_works": "Negativity – “Destroy the Entire EV Industry” is full of drama, which grabs people’s attention and makes them click.     {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_8_never-do-this-when-making-youtube-videos",
      "title": "I tried this mind-blowing ancient Japanese technique",
      "framework": "I tried this mind-blowing ancient (Culture) technique",
      "hook_score": 12537,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "Authority – “Ancient Japanese” grabs the audience’s attention and builds trust that this is a cool and effective technique. Curiosity – This opens a loop because it makes you wonder what the technique is and how the creator went from dirt on the left side of the thumbnail to the smooth, round ball on the right. Epic – “Mind-blowing” and “ancient Japanese” make this title feel epic. <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Try an ancient technique to do something interesting. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong>",
      "why_it_flopped": "",
      "view_count": "",
      "created_at": "2026-03-16 21:40:29"
    },
    {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_138_youtube-will-hate-me-for-sharing-this-secret",
      "title": "How the Most Underrated High School Recruit made the NFL",
      "framework": "How the Most Underrated (Entity) (Achieved Big Goal)",
      "hook_score": 11947,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "Curiosity – There’s contrast between “Most Underrated High School Recruit” and “made the NFL”, and it’s counterintuitive this would happen, too. Underdog – People love stories about underdogs. <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Tell the story of how the most underrated person achieved a big goal. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong> <a href=\"https://go.creatorhooks.com/103-youtube-title-a-b-tests\"></a>Title B adds a bit more drama and curiosity to get a higher CTR here. It opens a loop and makes you want to know what the 1 problem is. “Beautiful Caves” and “Problem” provide contrast for even more curiosity. And switching the focus of the title from “Not Learning My Lesson” to “1 Problem” makes it more tangible, specific, and dramatic. To use a similar framework for your channel, talk about a good thing that has 1 problem. If you want to see 100+ other test results like this, you can <a href=\"https://go.creatorhooks.com/103-youtube-title-a-b-tests\">​​get the 103 YouTube AB tests ebook here​​</a>. And if you want to run your own A/B tests, I used <a href=\"https://thumbnailtest.com/?via=jake\">​​thumbnailtest.com​​</a> to run this test (affiliate link). Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks!       "framework": "He secretly (Illegal Action), helped (Large number of entities)",
      "hook_score": 10610,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "Curiosity – This builds curiosity three ways: <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Tell the story of how somebody did something illegal that helped lots of people. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong> Click triggers are triggers that make people click. You can see different click triggers in the “Why this works” sections below each video in this newsletter. This week’s Click Trigger of the Week is Authority. There were two titles in this edition that used authority, but let’s dive into this a little bit deeper. Using authority in your titles can accomplish one or both of these goals: And there are two ways to use authority in your packaging. The first way is to use somebody’s name. This is best if a person is well known. Here are some examples: <a href=\"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zecjFAPHjVg\"></a> <a href=\"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Swx3sGAqngU\"></a>(This is actually uses both strategies, which leads us to the next one…) The second way is to use somebody’s position. This way is best if a person is not as well known, but their position holds a lot of authority. Here are some examples: <a href=\"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BVjT3dzVvbA\"></a> <a href=\"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q0uXGQu55GM\"></a>To use authority in your titles, drop somebody’s name or position to either build more credibility or get a little extra attention by fans of the authority figure. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks!       "newsletter": "newsletter_12_5-common-youtube-tips-that-can-ruin-your-channel",
      "title": "5 Common Medications That Can Damage Your Liver | Healthy Care",
      "framework": "5 Common (Solutions) That Can Damage Your (Possession)",
      "hook_score": 7071,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "List – Lists make videos feel more tangible (you know exactly what the video will be about) and they add some curiosity. Negativity – This leverages the fear of damaging your liver. And this fear is increased because these medications are common. Curiosity – There’s contrast between “common medications” (things that are assumed to be good for you) and “damage your liver.” <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Tell your audience about common things that cause a big problem. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong> Click triggers are triggers that make people click. You can see different click triggers in the “Why this works” sections below each video in this newsletter. This week’s Click Trigger of the Week is Open Loop. Open loops are the easiest and most common way to build curiosity. They’re made when you start a story, but don’t finish it. You might also know these as cliff hangers or curiosity gaps. Pretty much every title in this edition has an open loop, but here are a few more examples: <a href=\"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-_oaIgQF-e4\"></a>This makes you wonder what instrument they’re talking about. <a href=\"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RUe0o9VRgMM\"></a>This makes you wonder what happened to the Spotify Top 10 that made it worse and who’s in it. <a href=\"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uzhE4WxDlP4\"></a>And this makes you wonder what’s the leg workout he’s talking about. To use an open loop in your videos, start a story, but don’t finish it to make your audience feel compelled to click and watch your video. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.       "title": "How to STUDY so FAST that it feels ILLEGAL😳",
      "framework": "How to (Action or Goal) so FAST that it feels ILLEGAL😳",
      "hook_score": 4072,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "Desire – This creator’s audience wants to study fast. Speed – Adding speed (in this case, “study so <em>FAST</em>“) is a great way to enhance your audience’s desire and interest in your video’s topic. Negativity – “feels ILLEGAL” adds a bit of controversy and an edge to this video that makes it feel more exciting. <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Show your audience how to do something so fast that it feels illegal. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong> <a href=\"https://go.creatorhooks.com/103-youtube-title-a-b-tests\"></a>Suppose your video is about a topic people don’t immediately recognize or aren’t necessarily excited about (even if it’s cool once they learn about it). In that case, you might not want to include the exact topic in your title. Instead, you can zoom out and give it a simple, but extreme label (like “Ireland’s Creepiest Island”). This makes your title easier to understand and more exciting. If you want to see 100+ other test results like this, you can <a href=\"https://go.creatorhooks.com/103-youtube-title-a-b-tests\">​get the 103 YouTube AB tests ebook here​</a>. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks!       "hook_score": 3795,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "List – Lists make videos feel more tangible (you know exactly what the video will be about), and they add some curiosity. Curiosity – There’s a bit of curiosity here because you might assume it’s impossible for most cars to last 300,000 miles. <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Tell your audience about rules from an authority figure to achieve a seemingly impossible goal. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong>",

      "hook_score": 3755,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "Desire – This creator’s audience would love for every front to be free. Curiosity – This is counterintuitive because many fonts are <em>not</em> free. Negativity – This video talks about aging, which is a big problem this target audience is facing.     {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_12_5-common-youtube-tips-that-can-ruin-your-channel",
      "title": "What I Learned From My Failed Coffee Shop",
      "framework": "What I Learned From My Failed (Big Project)",
      "hook_score": 3463,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "Negativity – People love hearing about failure stories because they want to avoid it for themselves (among other potentially more negative reasons). Curiosity – This does a great job at opening a loop and making you want to know what he learned. Big Stakes – This might not have done as well if this was “What I learned from my failed coffee ebook,” but since this was such a big undertaking, it’s more interesting. <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Tell the story of how you failed at a big project. (Side note: here’s your encouragement to take on a big project without fear of failing because even if you “fail”, it could make for great content.) <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong>",

    {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_126_he-accidentally-started-a-youtube-channel-now-he-has-350000-subscribers",
      "title": "7 Signs of Undiagnosed Autism in Adults",
      "framework": "7 Signs of (Unrealized Trait) in (Specific Audience)",
      "hook_score": 3446,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "List – Lists make videos feel more tangible (you know exactly what the video will be about) and they add some curiosity. Confirms Suspicions – A lot of people are suspicious they might have autism, and this video invites them to watch and confirm (or refute) their suspicions. Negativity – In addition to confirming suspicions in some people, there might also be an element of fear for others. They might see this title and think, “Omg, do I have undiagnosed Autism? I need to watch this!” Specific Audience – By saying this is about “Autism in Adults” it makes it clear who this video is for, which makes adults who think they might have autism feel like this video was made just for them. <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Tell your audience about the signs of an undiagnosed or unrealized trait or problem that a specific group of them might have. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong> <a href=\"https://go.creatorhooks.com/103-youtube-title-a-b-tests\"></a>The original title does a good job at opening a loop and being clear, but there’s one thing title B does a lot better. First off, lists often do well in educational content like this. And I’ve seen “Every Day” work well often — I think it’s an underrated phrase that not enough people use in their titles. But the thing that these two click triggers (list and daily) do so well is they make the video more tangible. People love tangible titles because they tell you exactly what you’re going to learn. If you’re making educational content, you can use this strategy by telling your audience about the skills they should practice every day. If you want to see 100+ other tests like this, you can <a href=\"https://go.creatorhooks.com/103-youtube-title-a-b-tests\">​get my 103 YouTube AB tests ebook here​</a>. And if you want to run your own A/B tests, I used <a href=\"https://thumbnailtest.com/?via=jake\">​thumbnailtest.com​</a> to run this test (affiliate link). Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks!       "why_it_works": "Curiosity – “Could Change Forever” builds curiosity two ways. First, it opens a loop and makes you wonder, “What will change?       "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "List – Lists make videos feel more tangible (you know exactly what the video will be about) and they add some curiosity. Note: I typically don’t like using the word “and” in titles because it dilutes the focus, but here’s a great example of that being a preference, not a rule. <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Tell your audience about a list of things they should and shouldn’t be doing. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong>",

    {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_155_the-new-approach-to-youtube-that-changes-everything",
      "title": "Tax Expert Breaks Down Kamala Harris’s Tax Plan (FULL DETAILS)",
      "framework": "(Industry Professional) Breaks Down (Trending Authority Figure’s) (Relevant Entity)",
      "hook_score": 2881,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "Timeliness – This video was published a few weeks before the election and does a great job of riding the election’s popularity (and polarity).       "newsletter": "newsletter_11_give-me-5-minutes-and-ill-make-you-a-youtube-psychology-genius",
      "title": "How to DESTROY your exam like it owes you money (by a straight A engineering student)",
      "framework": "How to DESTROY your [Entity] like it owes you money (by a [Authority figure])",
      "hook_score": 1764,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "Desire – This creator’s audience would love to destroy their exam. Authority – This creator builds trust by dropping that he’s a straight-A engineering student at the end. Drama – Destroying your exam like it owes you money is a way more fun and dramatic way to say “get an A on your exam.” <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Teach your audience how to destroy something like it owes them money, and at the end, drop in why they should listen to you. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong> When you’re writing titles, it often helps to talk about things in broad terms. This makes it easier to read and understand. In this example, most people don’t know who Markelle Fultz is, but even if they did, they may not be interested, or it may take them a second to scan through their memory to remember who he is. “NBA Bust” is way more interesting, easier to read, and easier to understand. If you find yourself getting into the weeds in your titles, back it up a bit and give things broadly appealing labels so that your audience can quickly and easily know what your video is about.     {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_59_this-small-youtube-channel-actually-makes-big-money",
      "title": "This MICRO Canal is Actually Loaded w/ Fish? (Insane)",
      "framework": "This (Unimpressive Entity) is Actually (Unexpected, Desirable Trait)",
      "hook_score": 1132,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "Desire – This creator’s audience would love to find a canal loaded with fish. Curiosity – There’s contrast and counterintuitiveness here. You might think a micro canal (especially the one in the thumbnail that looks like it’s in a desert) would have no fish, but this title says it’s actually loaded with fish. <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Tell your audience how something small or unimpressive is actually really awesome. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong> Click triggers are triggers that make people click. You can see different click triggers in the “Why this works” sections below each video in this newsletter. This week’s Click Trigger of the Week is Timeliness. You might also know this as trend jacking or news jacking. It’s essentially just making content about what’s going on in the world or in your audience’s mind right now. But this is a tricky click trigger. The pro of using it is that it’s one of the most powerful ways to go viral. This is how MrBeast got 626,000,000 views on one video: He rode the Squid Game wave and it paid off huge for him. But the con is that once the trend or event is over, your views usually are, too. However, since this is such a powerful click trigger, the initial spike in views can be worth the drop-off later. Here are a few other examples of using timeliness in your titles: Dropping in the current or upcoming year: <a href=\"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BD9aVGh-vmc\"></a>(The above video was published in January of 2024.) Making content around a trending product or a big product release: <a href=\"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dtp6b76pMak\"></a>(This was published soon after the Apple Vision Pro was released.) Or making content about an upcoming event or holiday: <a href=\"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EhYIlntrxDs\"></a>(This video was published 2 weeks before Thanksgiving.) To use this click trigger in your titles, ask yourself what’s trending right now, or what is on your audience’s mind right now, and how can you make content around that. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks!     {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_111_always-like-your-youtube-videos-never-share-them",
      "title": "How Hanging Transforms Your Body",
      "framework": "How (Simple Action) (Big Result)",
      "hook_score": 1015,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "Desire – This creator would love to transform their body, especially by doing something as simple as hanging. Curiosity – This builds curiosity two ways: New Opportunity – Most videos in this niche are about exercises to help you achieve your goals. Hanging is new*, weird, and simple. If you haven’t tried hanging, and you want to transform your body, this new opportunity gives you hope because this might be the answer you’ve been looking for. *If you’re in this niche it might not be new to you, but to many people it’s new. <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Tell your audience that something new and simple can give them a big result. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong> <a href=\"https://go.creatorhooks.com/103-youtube-title-a-b-tests\"></a>Title B adds a price tag, which does two powerful things to make this title better: First, it makes the photography degree feel more tangible. Instead of just any degree, you know it’s an expensive, $6,000 degree. And second, it increases the stakes. If it was a free degree, you’re not losing much if it’s not worth it. But since the degree is $6,000, you’re risking a lot more if it’s not worth it. Making the title more tangible and the stakes higher both make title B more interesting, which helped increase the CTR here. To use this strategy for your channel, be specific in your title. Make it clear what your audience is risking or investing and how much they have to lose. If you want to see 100+ other test results like this, you can <a href=\"https://go.creatorhooks.com/103-youtube-title-a-b-tests\">​get the 103 YouTube AB tests ebook here​</a>. And if you want to run your own A/B tests, I used <a href=\"https://thumbnailtest.com/?via=jake\">​thumbnailtest.com​</a> to run this test (affiliate link). Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks!       "title": "Why UFOs are Suddenly EVERYWHERE",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": 305,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "Curiosity – This promises to answer a question a lot of people are asking themselves. Timeliness – UFOs are currently trending everywhere, so this is a hot topic <em>right now</em>. Negativity – UFOs are a scary and weird topic, so they grab your attention more than something more neutral, like trains. <strong>How you can use this hook:</strong> If something weird or scary is happening in your niche, explain to your audience why it’s happening. <strong>Examples of this hook in action:</strong>",
      "why_it_flopped": "",
      "view_count": "",
      "created_at": "2026-03-17 12:34:07"
    },
    {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_187_the-easiest-way-to-get-100000-subscribers-on-youtube",
      "title": "72 HOUR SURVIVAL (no food, no water, on an island)",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": 305,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "Constraints – Constraints like “no food, no water, on an island” build a lot of curiosity and conflict, which both make for an interesting video. Time Frame – “72 Hour Survival” lets you know exactly what this video is about, which makes you more likely to click. If it was just “SURVIVAL (no food, no water, on an island)” it would be a little more vague and less interesting. Negativity – This video addresses a common fear people have. People like to watch videos about things they fear and live vicariously through YouTubers. <strong>How you can use this hook:</strong> Take on a scary challenge with several constraints and a time frame. <strong>Examples of this hook in action:</strong>",
      "why_it_flopped": "",
      "view_count": "",
      "created_at": "2026-03-17 12:34:21"
    },
    {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_35_if-you-feel-your-youtube-channel-isnt-growing-read-this",
      "title": "Archeologists Uncover How Napoleon Lost At Waterloo",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": 303,

      "why_it_works": "Authority &amp; Credibility – Since archeologists “uncovered” something, it feels like this video has more authority and credibility than just a random historian’s opinion. Negativity – This is about drama — a famous person’s big loss — which people love. Curiosity – This also makes you wonder what they uncovered. <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Tell your audience how authority figures uncovered or discovered something dramatic. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong>",

    {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_96_the-fastest-way-to-100000-subs-from-any-starting-point",

      "newsletter": "newsletter_90_8-things-i-did-to-stop-sucking-at-youtube",
      "title": "When Helping An Upset Child, Don’t Talk &amp; Try This Instead",
      "framework": "When (Solving a problem), Don’t (Logical activity) &amp; Try This Instead",
      "hook_score": 18,
      "is_good_hook": "Yes",
      "why_it_works": "Negativity – This is all about solving a problem. Curiosity – This builds curiosity two ways: Editor’s picky note: if I was writing this title I’d use “but” instead of “&amp;” because typically “but” adds more conflict and tension than “and,” which makes for a better title. <strong>How you can use this framework:</strong> Tell your audience that when they’re solving a problem, don’t do a logical, assumed solution, but try “this” instead. <strong>Examples of this framework in action:</strong> <a href=\"https://go.creatorhooks.com/103-youtube-title-a-b-tests\"></a>“The Truth About” is a proven framework, so why did this title not win? Two thoughts: In this case, I tested a bold, opinionated, open-ended statement and it worked. Plus, the word “weird” often works well.     {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_38_if-youre-sick-of-your-channel-not-growing-you-need-to-read-this",
      "title": "50 AND FABULOUS: COSY COMFY STYLE FOR WOMEN",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -64,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "This title doesn’t really fit that. “50 AND FABULOUS” is too clever and vague, and dilutes the main idea of the title: “COSY COMFY STYLE FOR WOMEN.” Here are some other options we could try: We could try calling out a pain point and dropping in the current year: “How To Dress Stylish In 2025 (Without Being Uncomfortable)” We could add a little curiosity by making it about a secret: “The Secret To Dressing Stylish AND Comfy” Or we could use a similar format to the outlier above this section: “If you want to dress stylish and cosy, you need to watch this.” Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks!       "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "When I think about what makes a good title, I think about the three clickworthy emotions: Does articulating your thoughts more clearly to women you like spark any of these emotions? Maybe desire, but I think there’s a better way we could word it to evoke more emotion. Here are some examples for each one: <strong>Curiosity:</strong> “The 3-Second Rule Will Change How You Talk To Women Forever” <strong>Fear: </strong>“How To STOP Freezing When You Talk To Your Crush” <strong>Desire:</strong> “How To Make Any Woman Fall In Love With You” TLDR: Write clear and emotional titles about your audience’s fears and desires using simple words. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.     {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_55_to-blow-up-your-channel-always-do-this-before-every-video",
      "title": "The US is Falling Behind…",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -70,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "If somebody told me my baby was ugly, I wouldn’t listen to them. I don’t wanna hear it. I think that’s what’s happening here. I’m guessing this audience is proud Americans and they don’t want to hear the US is falling behind. Instead, it might be better to go with something more neutral, but still curiosity-building, like: “The US Economy Is About To Change Forever…” We could also use negativity, but just be negative about something else: “The Next 3 Months Look Terrifying…” These ideas still gets the same thought across without insulting the audience’s baby. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.       "why_it_flopped": "If it were me, I’d probably skip this topic and focus on more broadly appealing topics, like tips and mistakes of speaking current-day French. But since she already made this video, we could try another angle to write the title. Since the thumbnail is about the right way to cuss, we could follow that angle for the title and write something like: (I don’t particularly love these, I’d need to work them some more, but this is just an example of how you can go from broad and boring to specific and spicy.)     {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_78_5-simple-tips-to-unlock-youtube-easy-mode",
      "title": "Make This for Your Next Wine Night to Impress Everyone | America’s Test Kitchen",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -70,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "1) When you first glanced at the thumbnail, did you immediately know what it was? Maybe I’m just a simple man with simple tastes, but it took me a second to realize what I was looking at. It might’ve done better if it were a slightly clearer image of clams (they are clams, right?). Here’s a quote to remember when making your titles and thumbnails: “If you confuse, you lose.” 2) I also love that this title is about a specific moment: making food for wine night. But how many people in this audience are making food for wine night? It’s possible it’s not that popular of a moment, or the audience isn’t struggling to make food for that moment. If they wanted to get away from the wine night angle, they could try a title that just hypes up the clams, like, “These 10-Minute Steamed Clams Will Change Your Life”. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.     {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_115_5-life-changing-psychology-hacks-for-youtubers",
      "title": "How To Make More Money In Your Career!",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -70,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "It reminds me of one of my new favorite quotes: “Don’t listen to a customer, listen to your customers.” In this scenario, it means that if one person has a question, it might not be worth making a whole video about because it could be too small of an audience, but if a lot of people ask you the same question, then listen up because it may be a big question that many people have. I’m sure many people in this creator’s audience want to make more money, but I feel like $70,000 — the number from this specific question — just isn’t exciting enough to put in the thumbnail and intro. If it were me, I’d probably put $100,000 in the thumbnail because that feels like a big goal more people have and are really excited about hitting. Even the creator said in the intro “$70,000 today isn’t worth what it was even a year ago,” so he knows this number isn’t all that enticing. Ok, enough of me ranting about the thumbnail text. How can we make the title more exciting? Here are a few options: Deciding which one of these titles (or another) to go with really depends on your audience and the traffic source you’re targeting. Plus, you can see why it’s so important to come up with your title before you write your script because each of these titles is a different angle and, ideally, you’d reaffirm your title in the intro so the audience knows this video is right for them and they’ll keep watching. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.       "created_at": "2026-03-17 12:34:12"

      "why_it_flopped": "I’m not saying it’s not effective — it probably attracted buyers for the Pecron 1500 Pro and they probably got lots of affiliate sales. Plus, it’ll probably rank it search and do well in the long term. Buuutttt… they could’ve spiced it up a bit.       "newsletter": "newsletter_19_hate-writing-clickbaity-youtube-titles-this-is-for-you",
      "title": "The 1 Bass Spawning Behavior I DIDN’T Expect (Dock Cam Footage)",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -71,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "My guess is that although bass spawning behavior can be related to catching bass, it’s a few steps away. It takes a bit of mental work for the audience to realize that this video can help them with their main goal. And since most people are mindlessly scrolling on YouTube, any amount of mental work is often too much work, so they scroll on by and choose another video. A super simple change might be just: “The 1 Bass Behavior I DIDN’T Expect (Dock Cam Footage)” (just remove the word “spawning.”) You could also try a more dramatic title with a broader angle: “Underwater Video Changes Everything I Thought I Knew About Bass” TLDR: Know why people watch your videos and have your titles reflect that, and make your titles super simple to read and understand. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks!       "newsletter": "newsletter_46_93-of-youtubers-write-titles-the-wrong-way-heres-how-to-fix-it",
      "title": "How Resting Squats Completely Healed My Body",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -72,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "Well, maybe “resting squats” isn’t exciting enough. We could try this: “How This One Exercise Completely Healed My Body” This adds a bit of scarcity because it focuses on just one exercise and adds some curiosity because we didn’t specify what it is. It’s also possible that “Completely Healed My Body” isn’t powerful enough either. We could try this: “How This One Exercise Completely Changed My Life” Or “How This Simple Exercise Completely Changed My Life” Or “Doing This Simple Exercise Every Day Completely Changed My Life” We added some more curiosity and made it more broadly appealing by being more vague, but still focusing on the benefits. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks!     {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_19_never-do-this-in-your-youtube-titles-or-youre-done",
      "title": "Stop Guessing! Use This Cabin Trick to Avoid Scratches",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -74,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "I didn’t have any context when I first read “Stop Guessing!” and the exclamation mark told me that the first sentence was over, so for a split second, I was confused. Definitely not the first emotion you want someone to experience when they read your title. Unfortunately, as I kept reading, I got even more confused. I haven’t really heard the term “Cabin” much in reference to cars. I know what it means, but it’s just not a term I hear that often, so it took me a second to understand what I was reading. I’m not necessarily a car guy, so maybe I’m not the right audience, but ideally, your title is easily understood by everybody. So there are two things I’d fix here: Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.     {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_30_can-i-get-100000-youtube-subscribers-in-1-week",
      "title": "10 Minute Phrasal Verb Test: Can you pass?",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -74,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "If your audience doesn’t know what something is, they’re probably not going to click. This also might be a small potential audience issue. The first video in this newsletter about “200 Important English Expressions” is applicable to pretty much everybody learning to speak English, but how many people are looking to take a phrasal verb test? To avoid this mistake, use simple words in your titles and talk about topics with a large potential audience. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help with writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.     {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_153_5-signs-your-youtube-channel-is-about-to-blow-up",
      "title": "How To Thrift Like A Boss for Artwork and Home Decor | Julie Khuu",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -74,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "Most of the videos on her channel seem to be educational lists where the video has very clear value and structure. This thumbnail text tells you this video is a vlog, which may not fit what the audience is expecting and looking for from this creator. And to make things worse, the title and thumbnail are both busy and complex, which makes it hard to process when you’re quickly skimming through YouTube. The easiest way to make the title clearer and simpler is to focus on one thing. She could’ve taken out the “artwork” and just written: “How To Thrift Like A Boss for Home Decor”. I probably also would’ve taken out “like a boss” because it doesn’t really provide any value to the audience and their interests and instead, I would’ve doubled down on the thrifting part. Maybe something like this: “How To Thrift For Beautiful Home Decor Under $20” Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help with writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.       "newsletter": "newsletter_186_dont-make-your-next-youtube-video-until-you-read-this",
      "title": "My MASSIVE Reptile Enclosure has 23 Animals Preparing for a STORM!",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -74,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "This title violates that rule. This video is about “the coolest jungle enclosure you’ve ever seen” (according to the intro). Making the title about animals preparing for a storm is just trying to be too clever. If it were me, I’d make the title about how this is the coolest reptile enclosure you’ve ever seen. And I’d probably use the word “reptile” and not “jungle,” because reptiles are the reason people watch this channel. Now, you could ask if making a video about jungle enclosures was even a good idea in the first place, considering people watch this channel to learn about reptiles, but that’s a conversation for another email. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks!       "newsletter": "newsletter_1_5-things-smart-youtubers-should-never-do-on-their-channel",
      "title": "I Quit…",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -75,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "But this one did significantly worse than other videos on this channel. Why? Here are my two hypotheses: First, there’s a mismatch between the title and thumbnail. In an “I Quit…” video I would expect a more raw, emotional thumbnail with the creator front and center. Instead, we get this thumbnail. If I need hope (like the thumbnail text calls out), I don’t really want to watch a video where somebody else quit. My other theory is that this title is too vague. What did he quit? He gives zero context in the title, so we can’t know if this video is relevant for us. If the title was, “I Quit Feeling Bad For Myself…” then the thumbnail would work and the people it’s relevant for would know that this video is for them. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks!       "framework": "",

      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "And keeping that in mind, I think one of the reasons this video flopped is because making this iconic sandwich at home just isn’t a big problem for them. It might’ve been better to make the focus of the title about a different problem, like price or time: “Make This Iconic Sandwich For Under $5!” “Make This Iconic Sandwich In 5 Minutes!” There’s also the possibility that this creator’s audience is into big hunks of meats, not sandwiches, so maybe this video was doomed from the start.       "framework": "",

      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "We can’t really change #1 — the video just doesn’t have that much drama. And we can’t change #3 — the video is already posted and the audience wants what they want.     {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_96_the-fastest-way-to-100000-subs-from-any-starting-point",
      "title": "Screen time? Chore charts? Alone time? Clutter quandaries?!",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -75,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "My #1 rule of YouTube titles: keep them clear and simple, but 4 questions in a title don’t qualify as clear and simple. So what can we do? <strong>Option #1:</strong> Write the title addressing just the most interesting question. Example: “The #1 Rule That Keeps My Kids Happy Without Screen Time” <strong>Option #2:</strong> Hype up the questions. Example: “Answering Controversial Questions About Mom Life” Note: if you’re gonna do this, this obviously needs to be decided ahead of time, so you can pick controversial questions (or at least position the questions as controversial). <strong>Option #3:</strong> Use a time frame. Example: “32 Minutes of Advice For Struggling Moms” Or “32 Minutes Answering Difficult Questions For Struggling Moms” Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.       "newsletter": "newsletter_105_if-you-want-to-grow-your-youtube-channel-in-2024-try-this",
      "title": "This Chipping Technique Is So EASY And It Really Works!",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -76,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "Here are a few things I’d change: “It Really Works” is too vague and weak. What does it work for? Give me a juicy benefit that I’ll get using this chipping technique. Or <em>show</em> me that it really works, don’t tell me. Also, I don’t love using the word “And” in titles because it dilutes the focus and power of the title. Here are a few other options for a title: (Note that the original title has a positive angle, but some of the other options I suggested try curiosity or negativity. If one emotion doesn’t work very well, it can often be worth trying other emotions.) Another issue is that the title and thumbnail text are too similar: “simple” in the thumbnail and “easy” in the title pretty much reaffirm the same point. If it were me I’d either try a different benefit in the thumbnail text or try to add some curiosity or negativity. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks!       "title": "how i plan to reinvent myself and change my life in one year | level up",
      "framework": "",

      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "Negativity – This video is about a negative event, a recession, plus the thumbnail text talks about the end of an era with a scary-looking graph. Timeliness – This video rides a current trend because everyone is talking about a recession. Change – Although this isn’t about changing your life, here’s the word change again. Change helps open a loop, which makes your curious, and starts a story. <strong>How you can use this hook: </strong>Tell your audience about an event that will change a specific segment of an audience. <strong>Examples of this hook in action:</strong> <strong>Title: </strong>Let’s Discuss ADAPTING TOLKIEN! (Ft. The Script Department) | Middle-Earth Lore",

    {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_116_whats-working-on-youtube-in-august-to-grow-your-channel",
      "title": "A Day in the Life of Huw Richards",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -77,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "The video featured from this channel earlier in this email titled, “What Crops to Sow in June for Self-Sufficiency,” is a good example of this. But this video is creator-focused. This is a good strategy for people like Logan Paul, Emma Chamberlain, or other entertainment creators, but not as much for educational creators. One thing I would try is to position this video as a day in the life of what the audience’s dream is, like this: “A Day in the Life of a Full-Time Farmer” Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help with writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.       "view_count": "",
      "created_at": "2026-03-17 12:34:12"
    },
    {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_185_the-7-day-youtube-challenge-that-changed-my-life",
      "title": "What the Forest Taught Me About Gardening",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -77,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "The video is about lazy gardening (aka gardening without watering or weeding), and here are some of the previous titles she tried (thanks, Viewstats Chrome extension): It seems like she really only tried two angles: lazy gardening and what the forest teaches us about gardening. Here are a few ways we could mix it up: We could specifically call out the main benefit of lazy gardening: “The Secret To Having A Garden You Don’t Need To Water Or Weed” We could zoom out, be a little vague, and just hint at a big benefit: “This Gardening Secret Changed My Life Forever” Or we could add a hint of regret: “I Wish I Knew THIS About Gardening 10 Years Ago” Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.       "view_count": "",
      "created_at": "2026-03-17 12:34:21"
    },
    {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_8_never-do-this-when-making-youtube-videos",
      "title": "Are you having a complicated Christmas?",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -78,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "First, do you know what this video is about? Or who it’s for? It’s too vague. Keywords are often a debated topic when it comes to YouTube titles, but in this case, this title could use some keywords. Not necessarily for the algorithm, but just for the audience to know what the video is about. Also, this video was published on December 24th. Christmas is the very next day, so this video is irrelevant after that because people aren’t thinking about Christmas anymore. I’m all for trying to ride trends and current events, but this probably isn’t a strong and broad enough trend and they didn’t publish it early enough to make the most of the event. Plus, the majority of this channel’s content is evergreen, so they might’ve been better off spending their time making another evergreen video that addresses their audience’s timeless hopes, dreams, and fears. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.       "newsletter": "newsletter_4_top-5-new-youtube-trends-that-will-define-the-future",
      "title": "How A Domino’s Pizza Mascot Caused A Hostage Situation",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -78,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "The first is that it’s a complicated title. Specifically, “Domino’s Pizza Mascot” makes for a long and confusing subject. Compare this to the other video from this channel we featured earlier: “What Actually Is Blue Raspberry?” The subject “Blue Raspberry” is much simpler and easier to understand. Could they have just used, “Domino’s Mascot”? That might’ve been better. But there’s a bigger reason I think this flopped: it’s about a hostage situation. The audience watches this channel for food videos, not true crime videos. Even though this is technically about a food chain, the main story is still a crime story. To avoid these mistakes: Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks!       "title": "Should you Study SPANISH Alone or in a Group? | How to Improve your Spanish: Self-study vs Groups",
      "framework": "",

      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "(Question titles do better when they’re common questions everybody has asked themselves.) But by asking this question she’s excluding the people who think they don’t want to study Spanish in a group. It might’ve been better if the title was something like, “The Easiest Way To Learn Spanish FASTER.” This is a bold statement that adds curiosity and leverages desire, and gives her a chance to convince people who think they don’t want to study in a group to change their mind if they want to learn Spanish faster. (Of course, this is all assuming that it’s better to study in a group than alone.) In addition to the statement vs question bit, this title is super long and complicated.       "newsletter": "newsletter_9_why-growing-on-youtube-feels-impossible",
      "title": "Optimal Order Of Investing For Self-Employed",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -80,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "“Optimal” isn’t very clear or bold. A more clear, bold way to put it could be: “The Best Order Of Investing For Self-Employed.” It’s also possible this just isn’t a very exciting angle. We could add some more curiosity and excitement into this title like this: “Invest In This First If You’re Self-Employed” This title narrows the focus to just one thing thing to invest in first which makes it both more clear and more intriguing. Lesson: write clear, bold titles about exciting topics. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks!       "newsletter": "newsletter_23_how-to-instantly-improve-youtube-titles",
      "title": "The Surprising &amp; Unbelievable Dark Side Of Open Relationships: Aubrey Marcus | E242",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -80,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "This title makes me say, “There’s a dark side to open relationships? Duh!” There’s no curiosity here because it’s obvious there’s a dark side to open relationships. The best titles are non-obvious. They challenge the audience’s assumptions and show them a new truth. To avoid making this mistake, know what your audience believes to be true and in your title and thumbnail show them they’re actually wrong. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks!       "framework": "",

      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "Here’s what this video is about: Let’s brainstorm a few other titles. He could’ve focused on the constraint and scarcity that this is the single camera he uses to shoot photo and video at the same time: “The ONLY Camera I Use To Shoot Photo &amp; Video” But what if people aren’t interested in shooting photo and video with one camera? I’m not in this niche, so I don’t know if that’s the case, but if it is, we could go with a title like this: “If I Could Have Only ONE Camera, It Would Be This One” People love videos about settings, so another option is to only focus the title on the settings: “The Settings I Use To Shoot Photo &amp; Video With ONE Camera” Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! And always make sure your titles are clear. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks!       "newsletter": "newsletter_139_5-small-changes-to-improve-your-youtube-channel-forever",
      "title": "This Might Be The WORST Idea I’ve Ever Had…",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -82,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "If you’re going to use a vague title (like this channel did earlier in this email with the dirty car), the thumbnail needs to make it abundantly clear what your video is about. This video has a vague title and a vague thumbnail, which is a recipe to land in the flop of the week. It’s hard for your audience to be interested in a video where they don’t know what it’s about.     {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_164_the-one-choice-that-will-determine-your-youtube-channels-success",
      "title": "The Battle Between Elon, Trump &amp; The Left – Naval Ravikant",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -82,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "Specifically, I think there are too many nouns. Elon, Trump, The Left, Naval Ravikant — these four nouns make this title hard to digest. One thing they could do is take a stand and write the title about just one of the sides: “Why Trump Is Dominating The Left – Naval Ravikant” (I’m not saying that’s true or what this video is about — just using this as an example.) You could also argue that since this video has so few views compared to the rest of the channel, people aren’t interested in this battle. Part of this video is about power, and since this channel is about improving yourself, that could be an interesting focus for the title. The title could be something like, “Why Leaders Today Have More Power Than Any Other Man In History.” TLDR: Take a bold stance, or change the subject of the title if your video is struggling. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.     {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_180_these-types-of-videos-always-fail",
      "title": "6 things your (introvert) woman needs you to understand about dating her",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -82,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "“6 things your (introvert) woman needs you to understand about dating her” is kind of about the audience, but more about their woman. And although the audience cares about their woman, they care about themselves more. Not to mention, the fact that this is about dating introverted women narrows the audience a bit (which isn’t necessarily bad), but the audience needs to think for a second — is my woman an introvert? If I were to rewrite this title, I’d try to make it more about the audience: “If You’re Dating An Introvert You Need To Know THIS” or “6 Things You NEED To Know If You’re Dating An Introvert” Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks!       "framework": "",

      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "This video (and my second choice for flop of the week) seems to have a pretty good title. So this is just a reminder that the title isn’t everything. There’s a lot that goes into how successful you are on YouTube. My favorite bit of advice? Get 1% better with every video. Alright, that wraps up this week’s edition of Creator Hooks!       "view_count": "",

    {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_24_something-strange-is-happening-to-the-youtube-algorithm",
      "title": "Is Waymo About to Dethrone Uber?",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -83,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "In fact, it’s so good that a video by this channel using the same exact format got 405K views: “Is Chipotle About to Dethrone McDonald’s?” So why did this one flop, but the Chipotle video went viral? Here’s my best guess: most of us love Chipotle. We read that title and think, “Yes! Go Chipotle!” and then watch the video to see how they’re going to dethrone McDonald’s. But do people feel the same way about Waymo? Would you describe yourself as being on Team Waymo? Maybe I’m just a simple Florida man, but I don’t think so. And since nobody has strong emotions about Waymo, this title just doesn’t excite you as much as the Chipotle video. So, how else could we title this video? Well, we might not need to use Waymo in the title. We might be better off using an easily understood label instead and adding some more drama: “This Small Startup Is About To Ruin Uber” (Yes, I know Waymo isn’t small, but compared to Uber it is. We could also leave out the “small” if we wanted.) Or, we could focus the title wholly on Uber: “Uber Is About To Collapse” The lesson: modeling proven formats is the best way to come up with great video ideas, but ideally, you know exactly why the original video did so well, and your idea matches those conditions. Oh, and have a few backup titles just in case. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.       "newsletter": "newsletter_178_why-everyone-seems-to-be-better-at-youtube-than-you",
      "title": "WARNING: AI Is Stealing People’s Homes",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -83,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "My guess is that it’s not very believable. I’m writing this on my couch and having a really hard time imagining AI busting down my door and stealing my home from me. My house is just too tangible and I don’t think it’s going anywhere (although Hurricane Milton tried last year). I’m not saying the content in the video isn’t true, I’m saying just by reading the title I have a hard time imagining it to be. Now if this was about AI stealing my identity, bank account, or even people’s teslas out of their driveways, that’d be more believable because you can easily envision that happening. So the next time you’re deciding whether or not to make a video ask yourself: is this topic believable? Can people imagine how it would happen? Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.       "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "First, if we just wanted to focus on the title, given that this broad angle has flopped, we might want to zoom in and focus on a specific mistake, like “You’re Probably Gripping Your Driver Wrong — Here’s the Fix”. They don’t care about that — they just want to get better at golf. Third, your title and thumbnail don’t need to describe your video. In this case, “ai picks the lesson” and the ChatGPT logo are irrelevant — the viewer just wants to get better at golf. If the title and thumbnail were built around that, this video might’ve done better. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks!       "newsletter": "newsletter_2_the-youtube-tip-that-changed-my-life",
      "title": "What I Found in One of the Smallest Countries in the World",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -85,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "The biggest opportunity is to make the thumbnail and title work better together. The title sparks a question (“What did he find?”), but it feels like the thumbnail answers it with the image of the big furry bull/yak thing. In the “Photographers Who Got Caught Cheating” thumbnail above, there’s a little circle that the creator is pointing to that makes you say, “What’s that?” This creator could’ve done something similar. Instead of having the bull/yak, there could’ve been an arrow pointing to the city with text like, “So Weird” or something to make you wonder what he found. The title also could’ve been a little punchier. I don’t like using “one of the” in titles. Extreme things work well on YouTube and “one of the” waters that down. Instead, I try to make something the most extreme in its category. In this title, instead of “One of the Smallest Countries in the World”, I would’ve said “Europe’s Smallest Country”. If that doesn’t make sense or isn’t true, then I’d either try to put it in a different category or try a different angle. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.       "why_it_flopped": "First, the thumbnail text just repeats the title. This is wasting an opportunity to try another angle to get the viewer to click. The video featured in this newsletter earlier was about Canva tips, but Canva is so much more popular than Printify. Here’s a screenshot from Google Trends comparing the search volume of Canva vs. Printify: This creator has had a few successful videos about Printify, but they all seem to be search-based videos published a few years ago, while this has more of a browse-friendly title. If it were me, I’d either double down on search (something like, “How To Make Money With Printify In 2024”) or be more broad. I’d do this by talking about making money in general, instead of talking specifically about one tool, like Printify (ex: “10 Genius Side Hustle Ideas In 10 Minutes”).     {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_181_the-best-youtube-strategy-for-beginners-in-2023",
      "title": "$60 DIGITAL NOMAD hotel in Kuala Lumpur😁🙌🏼",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -86,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "Even if a $60 hotel is the “best hotel in KL” (like the thumbnail says) or expensive for KL, $60 is too average to grab most people’s attention. If it was a $5 hotel or $1,000 hotel, those prices would probably do a better job at getting the audience’s attention. Plus, these creators broke the “Show, Don’t Tell” rule in the thumbnail. They’re telling me in the thumbnail that it’s the best hotel in KL, but they certainly aren’t showing me (what am I even looking at?). Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help with writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.       "view_count": "",
      "created_at": "2026-03-17 12:34:21"

    {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_18_you-need-to-publish-bad-videos-heres-why",
      "title": "This Can Make or Break Your Retirement",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -87,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "It’s a cliche, it doesn’t take a side, and there’s no strong opinion. I think it might’ve done better if it were about either making retirement or breaking retirement, but not “make or break.” Here are some examples of what I mean: Making retirement: “5 Things That Will Make Retirement EASY” “99% Of People Don’t Know The Secret To A Great Retirement” Breaking retirement: “5 Things That Will Ruin Your Retirement” “99% Of People Are Accidentally Making This Retirement Mistake” These two sets of titles take a bolder stance and avoid clichés. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.       "view_count": "",
      "created_at": "2026-03-16 21:40:31"
    },
    {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_30_dont-build-a-youtube-channel-unless-you-can-handle-these-6-things",
      "title": "BEHIND ROCK’S MOST ICONIC GUITAR RIFF: Deep Purple",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -87,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "It might’ve been better if they wrote the title as “Behind Rock’s Most Iconic Guitar Riff” and in the thumbnail said something like “This Is Insane” or “This Is Genius!” That would’ve added a little bit more curiosity. They could’ve also added more curiosity or negativity, but I think that simple change would’ve done better with the current packaging idea. And then there’s the fact that this video is half as long as the video about Sting’s most copied riff. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help with writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.       "view_count": "",
      "created_at": "2026-03-16 21:40:32"

    {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_29_how-to-avoid-failing-on-youtube",
      "title": "Cheaper Than JDM: Great British Cars To Import Now",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -87,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "I’m not a car guy, but I know exactly what “Lamborghini’s NEW Active Alignment is Revolutionary!” (the title featured above from this same channel) means and I like Lamborghinis. I don’t know what JDM means and I’m not all that excited about great British cars because none easily come to mind. Again, I’m not a car guy, but if you want lots of views, you need to use simple language that lots of people easily understand and talk about topics that are interesting. Here are some of the other titles they’ve tried (thanks <a href=\"https://creatorml.com/?via=jake\">​CreatorML​</a>): It seems like “British cars” might not be the best phrase to go with here. They could go more broad and instead of focusing on the fact that these are British cars, emphasize that they’re exclusive and new to the US. Something like, “Classic Supercars Forbidden In The US”. But the thumbnail shows a cheap price tag, which doesn’t really fit with the title above. So they could either change the thumbnail to have text that goes with the title above, or they could change the title to go with the thumbnail text. Something like, “5 Affordable Classic Cars You’re Ignoring”. Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.       "view_count": "",
      "created_at": "2026-03-16 21:40:32"
    },
    {
      "newsletter": "newsletter_44_something-is-about-to-happen-with-youtube-are-you-ready",
      "title": "How To Clean Your Engine Bay For CHEAP!",
      "framework": "",
      "hook_score": -87,
      "is_good_hook": "No",
      "why_it_works": "",
      "why_it_flopped": "Now this could be an SEO play and this video may get lots of views over time. But also, sometimes you have to make a video about an unsexy topic. Maybe a sponsor requested it, a small part of your core audience begs for it, or it promotes a product you sell. So let’s brainstorm ways to make this title more clickable. We could make it more tangible, yet still add curiosity: “The $10 Cleaning Hack Every Car Lover Needs To Do” We could add some FOMO: “The Most Powerful Car Cleaning Step You’re Probably Not Doing” Or, if we’re sticking with the SEO game plan, we could be a little more specific about the benefits of this video: “How To Clean Your Engine Bay For $10 In 10 Minutes” Alright, that wraps up this week’s Creator Hooks! Hope you enjoyed this week’s edition and if you know a fellow creator who needs help writing better titles, please send them to <a href=\"https://creatorhooks.com/\">creatorhooks.com</a>. – Jake (<a href=\"https://twitter.com/jthomas__\">@jthomas__ </a>on Twitter) P.S.
